JACK I. HORTON
ANN MACKEY
CHIEF DEPUTIES

GERALD F. ADAMS
MARTIN L. ANDERSON
PAUL A. BECK

CHARLES A. DANIEL

ROBERT G. MILLER
JOHN A. MOSER
JIMMY M. MITCHELL
EUGENE L. PAINE
MICHAEL B. SALERNO
WILLIAM SHILL
WILLIAM K. STARK
MARK P. TETLEY
ROBERT E. THEIS
MICHAEL H. UPSON
RICHARD B. WEISBERG
ROBERT C. WEBB
THOMAS D. WHELAN
JAMES T. WHITEHOUSE
CRAIG R. WICKLAND
CHRISTOPHER ZIRKLE
DEPUTIES

Legislative Counsel
of California

3021 STATE CAPITOL
SACRAMENTO, CA 95814
(916) 445-3057

8011 STATE BUILDING
107 SOUTH BROADWAY
LOS ANGELES, CA 90012
(213) 620-2511
TELECOPER: 916-324-6311

BION M. GREGORY

Sacramento, California
January 17, 1990


Ms. Kathryn E. Donovan
General Counsel
Fair Political Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, CA 95804-0807

Dear Ms. Donovan:

As you know, this office is frequently asked to review
proposed mailings on behalf of a Member of the Legislature. One
issue which may be presented is whether certain materials are
"substantially similar" for purposes of Section 18901(c)(3)(A)(iii) of Title 2 of the California Code of
Regulations, as adopted by the commission on December 13, 1989,
relating to mass mailings sent at public expense.

In this connection, we have enclosed an example of three
letters which we believe may be viewed as outside the
"substantially similar" parameters and hence permissible to be
sent at public expense. We respectfully request your advices as
to whether these letters would be so characterized by the commission.

Very truly yours,

Bion M. Gregory
Legislative Counsel

By [Michael J. Kersten signature]
Michael J. Kersten
Deputy Legislative Counsel

MJK:jf

Much is in the news these days about "categorical programs."
These are programs designed to send more money to students with
special needs — for instance, those students who have
English language problems or are exceptionally gifted.

Unfortunately, funding for the categorical programs in
California has been skewed to benefit the large districts.
Money is not reaching students equally.

Schools in the Los Angeles Unified School District, for
example, receive $1,045 per student last year to support
categorical programs while schools get only

This disparity had to end. All students who are in need of
special training deserve to receive equal treatment in the
education system. That is why I led the
effort to pass a proposal that will equalize categorical
funding.

During the recent budget hearings, my colleagues and I
passed a new funding system which will begin to equalize the
old disparities. This new system will give schools
an additional _____ million in funds next year.

I'd appreciate hearing your comments or concerns about
categorical funding, or any other topic.

Sincerely,

Ensuring that our children receive a quality education is
certainly one of my top priorities as your state
with over $24 billion (more than half of the state budget) now
going to our public schools, I also want to make sure that your
tax dollars are spent fairly and wisely.

There is a series of "categorical" programs in our education
budget that is designed to help school children with special and
needed needs. These programs range from funding classes for the gifted and
talented kids to special classes for those who have difficulty
speaking English.

California currently has 75 categorical programs. This is a
major part of our educational effort, and is taking an
ever-increasing part of the education budget. However, a recent
study has shown that the funds for these programs are not
distributed equally for all districts.

The state average for categorical support is $464 per pupil.
Some districts, such as the Los Angeles Unified School District,
receive over $1,000 per pupil while
gets only

This disparity was not fair and needed to be corrected. That is
why I led an effort to equalize the funding for categorical
programs. This effort was successful, gaining approval by the
legislature as part of our budget hearings. Students in
can now expect an additional million in funding for the
1989-90 school year.

I'd appreciate hearing your opinion on this situation.

Sincerely,

Now that the Legislature has completed the education budget, I
thought I'd write to you and explain my efforts to remove the
unfairness built into the old funding scheme. Your children have
not been getting a fair share of the money the state spends on
education. I thought I'd report to you on what I've done to rectify
the situation.

In addition to the basic funding per student which is sent to each
school district, special interest groups have convinced the Legislature to earmark extra money for special
programs that are their "pet projects." While many of these
programs are laudable, the money has been divided unequally among
the schools.

In general, this specially earmarked money, called categorical
funding, has gone to the large, urban districts at the expense of
our suburban schools. For example, [illegible] schools got an average
of $1045 for each of their students. This just isn't fair.

As I developed a proposal to begin to equalize the per pupil spending.
My proposal will use some of the new money approved by the voters
to bring up to the statewide
with Proposition 98
average in categorical funding. My proposal has been adopted by the
Legislature, and we can now look forward to seeing a fairer system
about
of distributing education funds. This will give
more for our schools.

If you have any questions or comments, I'd like to hear from you.

Sincerely,
